coredata.dc:title: Enabling dual valorization of lignocellulose by fluorescent lignin carbon dots and biochar-supported persulfate activation: Towards waste-treats-pollutant
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Jun
Xu, Jikun
Liu, Xinyan
Dai, Lin
Zhang, Chuntao
Xiao, Xiao
Huo, Kaifu
coredata.dc:description: The rationally-designed lignocellulose valorization that promotes a novel “waste-treats-pollutant” standpoint is highly desired yet still challenging for the spread of biomass industry. At this point, a cascade technique with the assistance of deep eutectic solvent (DES) fractionation is tailored to dually valorize wheat straw into fluorescent lignin carbon dots (LCDs) and bimetallic Mg-Fe oxide-decorated biochar (MBC) via solvothermal engineering and co-precipitation/pyrolysis respectively. Benefitting from the abundance of β-aryl ether and hydroxyl groups in DES-extracted lignin, the photoluminescence LCDs emit blue color in a wide excitation span, which can be adopted to selectively detect ferric ions (Fe3+) in a broad dosage scale with a highly linear correlation of 10–50 μM. Taking advantages of the MBC-aided persulfate activation, we propose the efficient arbidol removal system with a universal concentration of 20–200 ppm in the scalable pH ranging from 3 to 11. The dominate migration pathways involving with active oxygen species and surface electron transfer are comprehensively studied via electron paramagnetic resonance, radical-quenching experiments, and theoretical arithmetic. With the endeavor of biorefineries, this full-scale platform ignites the dazzling wildfire from dual lignocellulose valorization that will also seek its accurate position in the kingdoms of functional materials and wastewater restoration.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of wheat straw valorization to enable fluorescent lignin carbon dots and biochar-supported persulfate activation. DES: deep eutectic solvents. LCDs: lignin carbon dots. PDS: peroxydisulfate (NH4)S2O8.

Structural characterization of DES-extracted lignin from wheat straw. (a) FTIR spectra of DES-extracted lignin (DES-Lignin) and lignin nanoparticles (LNPs); (b) the size distribution of LNPs; (c) the molecular weight distribution of DES-lignin; (d-e) structural characterization of DES-Lignin including side-chain (left, δC/δH 50–95/2.0–6.0) and aromatic regions (right, δC/δH 95–150/6.0–8.0) in the HSQC NMR spectra; (f) the main substructures of lignin identified in the 2D HSQC NMR spectra including β-aryl ether (A, β-O-4′), resinol (B, β–β′), phenylcoumaran (C, β−5′) linkages, syringyl (S), oxidized syringyl (S′), guaiacyl, (G), and free p-coumaric acid (pCA) units; (g) quantitative functional groups of hydroxyl and carboxyl in lignin. CS: condensed syringyl unit. CG: condensed guaiacyl unit. Total P-OH: the total content of phenolic hydroxyl; (h) sigma profiles of lignin model compounds (PP-ol, 2-phenoxy-1-phenylethanol; PP-one, 2-phenoxy-1-phenylethanone) and DES (choline chloride-ChCl and lactic acid-LA).

Physicochemical, photoluminescence, and optical sensing properties of lignin carbon dots (LCDs). (a) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence (excitation and emission) spectra of LCDs; (b) the size distribution of LCDs with an average diameter of 1.1 nm; (c) fluorescence emission spectra and (d) PL mapping image of LCDs; (e) fluorescence quenching response of LCDs with the presence of various metal ions; (f) fluorescence response of LCDs with the addition of Fe3+ ranging from 10 μM to 1200 μM; (g) relationship curve between the fluorescence intensity variation with the different concentrations of Fe3+; (h) linear plot between fluorescence variation and concentration of Fe3+.

Structural features of MgFe2O4-loaded biochar (MBC) from DES-treated wheat straw. Representative SEM images of (a) original wheat straw, (b) DES-treated wheat straw, and (c) MBC; energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) elemental mapping images of MBC including (d) C, (e) O, (f) Fe and (g) Mg; (h) XRD pattern of primitive biochar (BC) and MgFe2O4-loaded biochar (MBC), the (•), (★), (■) and (♦) represent MgFe2O4, Fe3O4, γ-Fe2O3 and MgO, respectively; XPS spectra of MBC: (i) survey spectrum, (j) C 1 s, (k) O 1 s, (l) Fe 2p, and (m) Mg 1 s.

Degradation efficiencies of the arbidol (ABD) solution at room temperature by varying (a) the catalyst-MBC (MgFe2O4-loaded biochar) amount, (b) the peroxydisulfate (PDS)-(NH4)2S2O8 dosage, (c) the ABD concentration, and (d) initial pH value. Reaction conditions: (a) [ABD]= 100 mg/L, [PDS]= 0.5 mM; (b) [ABD]= 100 mg/L, [MBC]= 2.0 g/L; (c) [PDS]= 0.5 mM, [MBC]= 2.0 g/L; (d) [ABD]= 100 mg/L, [MBC]= 2.0 g/L, [PDS]= 0.5 mM; (e) the comparison of removal efficiencies between arbidol (ABD) and methyl orange (MO) solutions. Reaction conditions: [ABD]= 100 mg/L, [MBC]= 2.0 g/L, [PDS]= 0.5 mM; (f) 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO)-trapped EPR spectra of SO4 •−and •OH with the time intervals of 0, 10 and 20 min; (g) 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidinol (TEMP)-trapped EPR spectra of singlet oxygen 1O2 with the time intervals of 0, 10 and 20 min.

Free radical mechanism for the arbidol decomposition. Quenching experiments (a) against reactive oxygen species via (b) gradient ethanol (EtOH), (c) tert-butyl alcohol (TBA), and (d) furfuryl alcohol (FFA). Adsorption structures and energies of PDS-(NH4)2S2O8 on (e) biochar and (f) MgFe2O4-loaded biochar (MBC) where C, O, S, Fe and Mg atoms are presented in gray, red, green, blue and yellow, respectively; (g) reaction paths and energy barriers for PDS dissociation on a biochar with MgFe2O4 bimetallic oxides to generate reactive oxygen species including •OH, SO4 •−, and 1O2. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Direct surface electron migration mechanism for the decomposition of arbidol (ABD). The changes of open-circuit potentials for MgFe2O4-loaded biochar (MBC) with the presence of (a) PDS and (b) ABD. Surface electronic structures and transfer number of PDS-(NH4)2S2O8 on (c) biochar and (d) MBC. The value of ∆q presents the electronic transfer number of PDS adsorbed on the surface of materials.

Proposed mechanism behind PDS-(NH4)2S2O8 activation over the MgFe2O4-loaded biochar (MBC) catalyst toward arbidol decomposition including three pathways of radical generation, singlet oxygen 1O2, and direct surface electron migration.

head.ce:title: Enabling dual valorization of lignocellulose by fluorescent lignin carbon dots and biochar-supported persulfate activation: Towards waste-treats-pollutant

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rationally-designed lignocellulose valorization that promotes a novel “waste-treats-pollutant” standpoint is highly desired yet still challenging for the spread of biomass industry. At this point, a cascade technique with the assistance of deep eutectic solvent (DES) fractionation is tailored to dually valorize wheat straw into fluorescent lignin carbon dots (LCDs) and bimetallic Mg-Fe oxide-decorated biochar (MBC) via solvothermal engineering and co-precipitation/pyrolysis respectively. Benefitting from the abundance of β-aryl ether and hydroxyl groups in DES-extracted lignin, the photoluminescence LCDs emit blue color in a wide excitation span, which can be adopted to selectively detect ferric ions (Fe3+) in a broad dosage scale with a highly linear correlation of 10–50 μM. Taking advantages of the MBC-aided persulfate activation, we propose the efficient arbidol removal system with a universal concentration of 20–200 ppm in the scalable pH ranging from 3 to 11. The dominate migration pathways involving with active oxygen species and surface electron transfer are comprehensively studied via electron paramagnetic resonance, radical-quenching experiments, and theoretical arithmetic. With the endeavor of biorefineries, this full-scale platform ignites the dazzling wildfire from dual lignocellulose valorization that will also seek its accurate position in the kingdoms of functional materials and wastewater restoration.

ce:section.ce:para: With the ongoing requirement of fuels and materials in anthropic daily life, it is highly imperative to explore sustainable technologies and merchandises with the objective of renovating modern chemical engineering (Li et al., 2021b; Xia et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2020). As a naturally renewable non-fossil organics containing inherent chemical energy, lignocellulose biomass is an emerging subclass of alternative resources with the huge potential to offset petroleum consumption (Antar et al., 2021; Liao et al., 2020; Robertson et al., 2017). Besides, it is widely proposed that lignocellulose can be engineered as the impactful medium to reduce CO2 emission and atmospheric pollution with the perspective of carbon-neutrality (Luterbacher et al., 2014; Pang et al., 2021a). Over the past few decades, the prevalent procedures of lignocellulose biorefining are preferentially dependent on the carbohydrate valorization to allow for the implementation of paper-making and bioethanol industries. During a typical biorefinery, biomass pretreatment is a crucial step which could break down the natural recalcitrant structure of lignocellulose and dissociate its three building blocks (i.e., cellulose, hemicelluloses, and lignin) (Shuai et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2021a). Selecting a task-specific and benign medium that suitable for the biomass fractionation is generally deemed as a powerful strategy to enable the industrial sustainability and feasibility of future biorefineries. Due to their favorable physicochemical advantages, e.g. recyclability, negligible vapor pressure, non-toxic and low-cost characters, deep eutectic solvents (DES) that can be easily modulated from a wide variety of hydrogen bond acceptors (HBA) and hydrogen bond donors (HBD) have emerged as the intriguing green reaction solvents for lignocellulose processing (Liu et al., 2021).

Because of their cross-linked and complicated structure, the holistic utilization of lignocellulose components to worthy-application items is still a worldwide challenge (Pang et al., 2021b). Note that, one-pot lignocellulose fractionation is technically troublesome to realize waste-free valorization due to the contradictory properties of lignin and carbohydrates, i.e., cellulose and hemicelluloses are favorable to upgrade while lignin is stubborn to utilize. In order to push forward the multi-scale biomass valorization, task-specific deconstruction coupling with cascade-step regime has been widely proposed to meet the demand of integrated lignocellulose applications. For instance, a tandem technique of liquid hot water followed by DES extraction was constructed to sequentially deconstruct lignocellulose, thereby delivering three process streams that can be further valorized to multiphase nanomaterials (i.e., activated nanocarbons, lignin-containing cellulose nanofibers, and lignin nanoparticles) (Tian et al., 2022). Recently, a brand-new and versatile EXA biorefinery strategy was developed to prepare bioethanol, xylose, and lignin adhesive as ultimate products from corncob lignocellulose (Pang et al., 2021a). It is an undeniable fact that the homothetic “waste-free” biomass utilization would not only reap the immense financial rewards from biorefining, but also boost the global value and low-carbon footprint materials and/or chemicals in the foreseeable future.

The valorization of the plant-structured biopolymer into advanced environmental materials is now recognized as essential to enabling the circular water economy of lignocellulose valorization (Chen et al., 2021; Jiao et al., 2022a, 2022b). In view of this, a dubbed “waste-treats-pollutant” approach, in which wheat straw is considered as original substance during the design period, has demonstrated the multiphase water-pollutant control by agricultural waste-derived sustainable materials (i.e., fluorescent LCDs and catalytic MBC). Herein, we successfully strides the threshold of dual lignocellulose valorization based on a novel “waste-treats-pollutant” concept which guides the orthodox biomass industries to serve environmental engineering (e.g., heavy metal ions and organic contaminants) via a DES-driven cascade scheme. This easy-to-operate fractionation technique derives the overall revenue from classical DES pretreatment of lignocellulose: (1) DES-isolated lignin, which is facilely converted into carbon dots (LCDs) as the photoluminescence nanoprobe for recognizing and sensing metal ions in wastewater; (2) leftover DES-treated wheat straw, which can be loaded with bimetallic oxides to prepare biochar (MBC) with the practical ability of catalytic persulfate activation for decomposing arbidol drug pollutant. Particularly, well-defined structure of DES-extracted lignin is dissected to seize insights into how this coupled procedure benefits the functions of fluorescent LCDs and catalytic MBC. We thus envision that it could not only open a window to support the current lignocellulose biorefinery via building a tandem platform from multidimensional lignocellulose valorization, but also create a bright prospect to synchronously remediate intricate wastewater in terms of sensing and/or degrading hazardous materials.

Increasing the revenue from three building blocks in lignocellulose feedstock is of tremendous potential to rejuvenate traditional biorefinery technique (Alonso et al., 2017). Herein, a cascade-step strategy with an initial DES fractionation is proposed for the dual biomass valorization into lignin carbon dots (LCDs) and bimetallic MgFe2O4-loaded biochar (MBC), delivering the opportunity to propel a “waste-treats-pollutant” thinking in environmental wastewater remediation via fluorescent sensing of heavy metal ions and biochar-supported persulfate activation of arbidol degradation. As illustrated in Fig. 1, typical lignocellulose of wheat straw was firstly treated with DES system composed of choline chloride (ChCl) and lactic acid (LA), offering DES-extracted lignin and leftover carbohydrate-rich substance. This fractionation step preserves the value of complete lignocellulose conversion: (1) lignin, which can be served as a well-defined precursor for preparing fluorescence CDs in the presence of DES; and (2) the residual straw was decorated with bimetallic oxide (i.e., MgFe2O4) to engineer biochar-supported PDS activator for the catalytic degradation of medical wastewater (i.e., arbidol). The primary intention of this research is to create the feasibility of the integrated scheme for generating photoluminescence nanomaterial and Fenton-like catalyst toward the biorefining manufacture at a large-scale environmental application.

It is witnessed that DES occupies the excellent solvent strength and ability to break the strong interactions between lignin and carbohydrate complexes, thereby creating a profitable situation for the improvement of lignin extraction and processing. It also is of importance to broaden the frontier of biorefinery, in which lignin macromolecules as a process stream should be potentially exploited in a rational manner. Efficient isolation of lignin fractions with a well-defined structure is an essential prerequisite for subsequent conversion at this level.

The Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) is a common technique to identify the functional groups grafted on biopolymers via the measurement of vibrational modes. To be specific, some bands can be assigned with high confidence in the range of 1000–3000 cm–1 (Ma et al., 2020). As depicted in Fig. 2a, the lignin spectra exhibited an intense and broad band at 3400 cm−1, which is attributed to the O–H stretching in aliphatic structures. The absorption of C–H asymmetric and symmetrical vibrations from methylene (–CH2) and methyl (–CH3) groups is located at around 2950 cm–1, and the weak peak of 2860 cm−1 is corresponded to the methoxy (–OCH3) groups. A stretching peak at approximately 1730 cm−1 can be contributed to the presence of unconjugated carbonyl and conjugated ester bond formed by phenolic acids. In particular, the aromatic skeletal vibrations are explicitly confirmed by the bands at 1600, 1510 and 1425 cm−1, enabling a cardinal phenylpropanoid structure of lignin (Ma et al., 2020). The absorbance at 1470 cm−1 also corresponds to the aromatic skeletal vibrations and C–H deformations. Another absorbance at about 850 cm−1 is strongly related with aromatic out-of-plane C–H bending. Furthermore, the presence of distinctive peaks corresponded to syringyl units (1330, 1220 and 1110 cm−1) and guaiacyl structures (1270 and 1030 cm−1) in lignin molecules are also observed. Meanwhile, the lignin nanoparticles (LNPs) can be easily prepared via a modified acid precipitation method (Ma et al., 2019). The FT-IR spectra between DES-lignin and LNPs reveal similar patterns, with a few varieties about the relative intensity of specific absorbance, suggesting the negligible changes in lignin structure during LNP preparation stage. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) result indicates that the LNPs are well-dispersed with an average size of 242.61 nm (Fig. 2b). This phenomenon is mainly ascribed to the specific macromolecular framework and solubility of the DES-extracted lignin, which should aggregate via robust π-π stacking interaction and abandon its hydrotropic property to self-assemble into regular nanoparticles under the acidic environment (Ma et al., 2020). The molecular weight distribution and polydispersity detected by gel permeation chromatography (GPC) are listed in Fig. 2c. It is well known that the initiation of polymeric aggregation could be affected by molecular weights and chain entanglement. Herein, the DES-extracted lignin exhibits a weight-average molecular weight (M w) of 1510 g mol–1 (< 5100 Da), which is obviously lower than that of the common synthetic polymers. Therefore, the influence of side-chain conformation on the LNPs fabrication might be neglected (Zhao et al., 2017). Of note, the GPC analysis also confirms that the DES-lignin presents its heterogeneous structure with a narrow polydispersity (M w/M n =1.3), implying the relatively uniform precursor for fluorescent CDs.

Precious lignin structure dominates its ultimate properties of materials. We adopt in-situ nuclear magnetic resonance techniques (2D-HSQC and 31P NMR) to gain fundamental chemical details (e.g., interior linkages, –OH and –COOH) of lignin, which will build a bridge between individual lignin characters with photoluminescence CDs. The 2D-HSQC spectra of lignin and its typical substructures are illustrated in Fig. 2d-f. From the side-chain region of the DES-lignin spectrum (Fig. 2d), the typical substructures and interunit linkages, including methoxyls (–OCH3), β-O-4′ aryl ethers (A), resinols (B), phenylcoumarans (C), could be clearly discriminated with the reference of recent works (Liu et al., 2021; Wei et al., 2021). In other words, the cardinal lignin linkage of β-O-4′ (substructure A) capable of high abundance is readily determined, being accompanied by the presence of interlinked β-β′ (B) and β-5′ (C). A highly linear interplay between lignin β-O-4′ linkages with its carbonaceous crystallite structure and physicochemical properties has been authenticated (Li et al., 2021a). It thus is emphasized that such a well-defined correlation would direct the rational design of chemical lignin processing towards high-quality lignin carbon nanodots. As presented in the aromatic region (δC/δH 95–150/6.0–8.0) of Fig. 2e, the intense cross-signals from syringyl (S), oxidized (Cα=O) syringyl (S′), and guaiacyl (G) units are sharply differentiated. It has been affirmed that p-coumaric acid (pCA) is frequently acylated at C-γ of side chain in grass lignin (Wen et al., 2015). During the moderate DES-acidic environment, the extraction of leftover p-coumaric acid is validated by individual pCA resonance (C2,6−H2,6) at around δC/δH 130.0/7.45 (Wen et al., 2013).

As indicated in Fig. 2 g, the contents of aliphatic OH, COOH and phenolic OH (total P-OH) grafted onto lignin molecules are calculated to be 4.583 mmol/g, 0.312 mmol/g and 1.532 mmol/g, respectively. The main species of phenolic hydroxyls appears to be guaiacyl OH (G-OH, 0.698 mmol/g), followed by syringyl OH (S-OH, 0.534 mmol/g). A trace content of p-hydroxyphenyl OH (H-OH, 0.01 mmol/g) in lignin is detected, suggesting that the H substructure is hard to be extracted via DES solvents. The condensed-OH contents of CS-OH and CG-OH are 0.178 mmol/g and 0.112 mmol/g respectively, which potentially provides an attestation for the partial occurrence of lignin condensation during acidic DES pretreatment. More importantly, the presence of oxygen-contained groups (e.g., –OCH3, –OH, and –COOH) in lignin precursor would in-situ doping into CDs, which is propitious to hybridize carbonaceous corn (e.g., the sp 2/sp 3 proportion and intrinsic defect) and modulate surface active sites (e.g., oxygen-induced edge and vacancy) (He et al., 2022). Hence, the core-shell structure and photoluminescence properties of LCDs can be regulated via the oxygen-contained groups of lignin.

It has been witnessed that DES efficiently solubilize lignocellulose and extract lignin to depress their natural recalcitrance for the items of biofuels and chemicals (Ma et al., 2022a). Theoretical explicating the solubility of lignin biopolymers in appropriate DES is of great concern to select HBA and HBD for lignocellulose pretreatment (Mohan et al., 2022). The solubility parameter of a molecular species is one of the pivotal characteristics that determines the polarity/non-polarity and quantifies the like-seeks-like principle, which is often investigated in the sphere of biopolymer dissolution to elaborate the potential extraction mechanism. Herein, we select 2-phenoxy-1-phenylethanol (PP-ol) and 2-phenoxy-1-phenylethanone (PP-one) as the representative lignin model compounds to illuminate their soluble performance in the mixture of ChCl and LA (Mohan et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2014). As can be seen from Fig. 2 h, the sigma profile of PP-ol and PP-one exhibits the main peaks in the non-polar region (–0.01 e/Å2 < σ < 0.01 e/Å2), which illustrates that the lignin models are peculiarly prone to be dissolved in non-polar DES molecules. Due to the presence of hydroxyl group, the studied DES solvents exhibit peaks in the non-polar region and two polar regions (HBD σ < –0.01 e/Å2 and HBA σ > 0.01 e/Å2) (Hu et al., 2021). The protic solvents possess the potential capacity to donate the partial charges that interrupt the interior linkages between lignin and carbohydrates (e.g., hydrogen bond interaction), thereby leading to an acceptable solubility for lignin removal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing properties of lignin carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Benefiting from of the modular design, DES are promising alternatives for the intercalation of supplementary functions (e.g., the heteroatom doping and stabilization action), which are highly desired in nanomaterial engineering and/or lignocellulose biorefining. Photoluminescence CDs are an emerging subclass of nanoparticles with a characteristic size of < 10 nm and a quasi-spherical morphology. Through a solvothermal engineering, CDs are usually defined by a carbon-oriented core and the possibility to engineer various functional groups at the surface for fluorescent sensing applications (Ðorđević et al., 2022). It is worth forecasting that lignin seems to be irresistible for fabricating CDs because of its aromatic phenylpropanoid substructures, high carbon content, and abundance functional groups, which offers a synergistic occasion of resembling the carbon skeleton and surface decoration of CDs with a lignin-M w related nanoscale size (Wang et al., 2019b; Xu et al., 2021b). In this vein, we adopt a DES-induced lignocellulose fractionation coupling with an extended solvothermal procedure to directly prepare functionalized CDs from lignin and DES, which deliver an explicit fluorescence quenching in response to the presence of Fe3+ with a broad concentration range of 10–1200 μM.

With the excellent dispersibility in aqueous solution, the optical properties of the as-prepared LCDs are discussed by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy in detail. As shown in Fig. 3a, the absorption spectrum of LCDs presents a band at around 275 nm, which is mainly ascribed to the π→π * transition of conjugated CC groups from sp 2/sp 3 hybridized carbon core (Zhang et al., 2021a; Zhu et al., 2021). In response to 400 nm excitation, an intense peak at maximum 490 nm with a Stokes shift of 90 nm is presented in the fluorescence spectrum, which is considered to be correlated with the specific quantum diameter, carbon core structure and electronic environment (e.g., crystalline region and sp 2/sp 3 degree), and diverse surface defects (e.g., oxygen, nitrogen, and chloride-doped groups) (Ai et al., 2021). The negatively charged LCDs (–1.85 mV) are homogeneously distributed in the narrow range 0.8–1.5 nm with an average diameter 1.1 nm (Fig. 3b). A previous work claimed that a smaller size with lower degree of graphitization on the carbon core is conducive to the blue shift of photoluminescence emission wavelength (Zheng et al., 2021). At this point, the inset image of Fig. 3a depicts that the color of aqueous LCDs solution is obviously transferred from colorless under visible light to bright blue fluorescence with UV lamp of wavelength 360 nm. Fig. 3c presents the fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs at diverse excitation wavelengths with 10 nm increment ranging from 340 nm to 490 nm. Taking advantaging from special quantum confinement, the LCDs possess an excitation-dependent emission behavior that is a typical character of carbon nanoparticles (Liu et al., 2020). With the increase of excitation wavelength, the corresponding emission spectrum gradually evolves into a red-shift wavelength accompanying the reduced intensity. Meanwhile, a maximum fluorescence intensity is located at the excitation wavelength of 410 nm. As illustrated from Fig. 3d, three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence profile of LCDs also provide a direct information about the relationship between their excitation and emission wavelengths. The alternating emission band of LCDs can be analyzed with the unfixed excitation wavelength, which is provoked from the energy gap trap of surface-localized states. That is to say, the photoluminescence behavior highly relates with CDs’ surface defects that might conquer size-dependent interband transition (Liu et al., 2017a; Wu et al., 2019).

It is inferred that the aromatic skeleton of lignin phenylpropanoid substructure might hold the capacity to coordinate with heavy metal ions such as Fe3+ (Ananthanarayanan et al., 2014). In this context, we speculate that DES-functionalized LCDs would be adopted as fluorescent nanoprobe to visualize wastewater quality via selectively recognizing the varied concentration of metal ions, owing to the unique structural of DES-lignin-derived CDs. Different species of familiar metal ions, including Cu2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+,Co2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Cd2+ and Hg2+ of each at the concentration of 500 μM, were blended with as-prepared aqueous LCDs solution (Fig. 3e). We can easily conclude that the as-prepared LCDs hold strongly sensitivity of Fe3+ via normalized PL intensity, while other metal ions cannot be able to implement the definitely photoluminescence quenching performance with respect to free LCDs. It follows that the binding affinity of Fe3+ with LCDs-derived electron-donating groups is stronger than that of other metal ions, enabling the rapid charge transmission and constrained recombination of excitons to drive a sharper decline of PL intensity (Wang et al., 2021). Under the optimized concentration, the fluorescent titration quenching is plotted to capture a dose-dependent behavior, which is presented in Fig. 3f-g. With the concentration of Fe3+ increased gradually, the blue emission at 470 nm reduced significantly in a wide concentration span of 10–1200 μM. It is conceivable that Fe3+ ion functions as a homocoupling evocator to interlink several LCDs together via the coordination with –COOH/OH groups on the LCDs edge, thereby leading to photoluminescence quenching action as consequence of the aforementioned LCDs aggregation. Affecting by the coordination interaction, it seems that the migration of LCDs-derived excited-state electron into the unfilled orbit of Fe3+ has taken place for the sake of non-radiative electron/hole recombination, which occupies a direct nexus of significant fluorescence quenching (Liu et al., 2017b). As shown in Fig. 3 h, the linear correlation for Fe3+ with the relationship of y = 0.0052x + 0.03666 (a coefficient of determination signifies line fitting, R 2 =0.984) is determined with the range of 10–50 μM. As compared with the other metal ions, it is reasonable to postulate that the CQDs capable of Fe3+ identification is partially initiated through the synergistic influence of quick electrostatic attraction and chelating kinetic, as well as robust affinity interaction between Fe3+ with functional groups grafted onto the LCDs surface (Bai et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2022). These findings could pave an avenue for integrating such emerging 0D nanoscopic biomaterials with pollutant monitoring function into the traditional biorefining systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural features and catalytic performance of MgFe2O4-loaded biochar
ce:section.ce:para: It is conceived that the biochar-based catalysts represent a promising class of adsorption and degradation systems, which generally comprise a carbonaceous skeleton coupling with exogenetic dopants. Their physical patterns, structural characters, and interfacial engineering are of the essence to judge the ultimate catalytic performance of biochar (Wang et al., 2019a). In the present research, the potential contribution of Mg leads to an improvement in the generation of Fe-dominant reactive radicals and/or intermediates for oxidative arbidol degradation. Especially, bimetallic magnesium ferrite (MgFe2O4) can be accepted because of its high catalytic activity, low metal ion leakage and facile magnetic separation (Qin et al., 2020). The epigenetic morphology, bimetallic loading status, and elemental mapping results of biochar are monitored by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), as shown in Fig. 4. The origin wheat straw encounters a representative rod-like fibrous structure with a compact and smooth surface (Fig. 4a). It is fortunate that the initial DES pretreatment is conducive to strip the flake-like and loose structure of plant cell wall, thereby creating the regular interspaces to loading the bimetallic oxide nanoparticles (Fig. 4b). After the facile MgFe2O4 co-precipitate approach (Fig. 4c), the dense and uniform distribution of the doped particles can reduce the mass transfer resistance and expose abundant active sites for the sake of the subsequent persulfate activation. As verified from Fig. 4d-g, the elemental mapping results also reveal that Fe and Mg elements are embedded in the biochar skeleton homogeneously. It is thus draw a fact that DES-treated straw functions as a biochar carrier to successfully fabricate a highly dispersed Fe-Mg bimetallic species, which is expected to promote the activity of catalytic persulfate activation. As presented in Fig. S1, the DES deconstruction is mainly conducive to punch the interfacial holes on the fibers of wheat straw (12.02 nm vs. 16.32 nm), whereas it has slight influence on the BET surface area of wheat straw (1.02 m2/g vs. 1.23 m2/g). After bimetallic loading of MgFe2O4, the BET surface area largely increased from 1.23 m2/g to 56.64 m2/g, while the pore size decreased from 16.32 nm to 8.65 nm. This result indicates that the biochar is capable of a mesoporous material with considerable adsorption capacity, and the loading of bimetallic oxides greatly enlarge the specific surface area (Qin et al., 2020). On the flip side, the intercalation of bimetallic Mg, Fe-oxides shaping in agglomerates has taken place within the pores to trigger an unavoidable reduction of average pore size from MBC (Fig. 4c). It is generally appreciated that Raman spectra holds the ability to depict the molecular lattice vibrational energy level and rotational level structure (Zhao et al., 2018). It can be observed that the graphene-like layer is formed through pyrolyzing Fe, Mg-codoped carbonaceous framework at the elevated temperature. Fig. S2 exhibits the Raman spectra of MBC, in which the response of D and G bands is centered at two strongly characteristic peaks of 1350 and 1600 cm–1 respectively. Broadly speaking, the D-band represents the sp 3 hybridization, atomic lattice deficiency, and the rings breathing of carbon atoms. The location of G-band is often adopted to mirror the sp 2 hybridization and E2 g C-sp 2 atomic vibration modes of C atoms (Zhao et al., 2018). The concomitance of D-G bands indicates that the MBC seizes not only ordered graphite structure but also irregular amorphous carbon during the pyrolysis procedure.

Of note, the regular graphitic texture could enhance the conductivity, electron transfer, and structural stability of carbonaceous skeleton. On the other hand, disordered curly structure would expand specific surface area, immobilize bimetallic particles, and disperse active spots. Furthermore, the intensity proportion of D-band to G-band (I D/I G=0.6) is generally reckoned to quantitatively identify the high graphitization degree of carbon substances comprising limited amorphous state. XRD spectra of BC and MBC are compared in Fig. 4 h, which demonstrate that Fe and Mg are successfully embedded in the composite with an increased ratio of oxygen species. In order to annotate the chemical environment and valence state of interfacial metal-oxygen-carbon linkages, the in-depth XPS spectra of MBC are conducted as shown in Fig. 4i-m. The full spectrum of Fig. 4i affirms the presence of magnesium, iron, carbon and oxygen. The C 1 s XPS spectra (Fig. 4j) are easily divided into three characteristic peaks located at approximately 284, 286, and 289 eV, corresponding to graphitic sp 2 carbon (C–C/CC), C–O–C/C–OH, and O–CO linkages, respectively (Yao et al., 2022). As can be seen from Fig. 4k, the location of C–O peak is centered at 533 eV, while 532 eV and 530 eV can be assigned to the Mg–O–C/Fe–O–C and Mg–O/Fe–O bonds, respectively (Qin et al., 2020). Deconvolution of the Fe 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 4 l) of MBC illustrates the two representative Fe species, in which Fe2+ bands are mainly resolved at around 710 eV and 723 eV, as well as, Fe3+ peaks is located at about 712 eV and 725 eV. The relative amount of Fe3+ is higher that of Fe2+ (75.81% vs. 24.19%), indicating that the γ-Fe2O3 is prone to deposit in the matrix of biochar. The fitting curve of Mg 1 s signifies that the Mg species imbedded into carbon layers is presented as the modality of MgO. From the above structural characterizations, it is convinced that bimetallic Fe, Mg-oxides implanted biochar catalyst is successfully fabricated to foster catalytic activity of subsequent organic degradation with the presence of persulfate.

Arbidol that acts as a potential anti-influenzal drug to clinically inhibit SARS-CoV-2 is advised for the treatment of COVID-19 within the national prevention and treatment guidelines (Padhi et al., 2021). Unfortunately, antiviral residues are ubiquitous in surface water, wastewater and groundwater worldwide, which pose ecological threats to anthropic activities and aquatic organisms (Gao et al., 2022). Herein, pharmaceutical pollutant elimination using persulfate as an oxidant is systematically conducted to rationally evaluate the catalytic ability of the as-prepared MBC. The catalytic performances and reaction kinetic constants of MBC-aided persulfate activation for arbidol degradation are presented in Fig. 5 and Fig. S3, respectively. With the increased dosage of MBC catalyst, the degradation efficiency of arbidol is gradually enhanced. The optimal amount of MBC is determined to be 2 mg/mL with arbidol removal rate of 94.8% in 60 min (Fig. 5a). As can be observed from Fig. 5b, the incremental addition of persulfate has no significant influence on the arbidol migration with the fixed concentration of 100 ppm. However, the limited arbidol removal of 47.8% without the presence of persulfate indicates that the catalytic performance can be attributed to the effective interaction between persulfate and MBC-catalyst with the adjunction of (NH4)2S2O8 (PDS), which is conducive to the degradation of organic pollutants through multiple radical and non-radical paths (Chen et al., 2018). The ultrahigh arbidol removal is clearly confirmed by the fact that the degradation efficiency exceeds 95% with the arbidol concentration of 20–150 ppm (Fig. 5c). The acceptable degradation of 83.6% is also achieved even at arbidol dosage of 200 ppm, thereby suggesting the efficient organic pollutants elimination by catalytic MBC system with high concentration wastewater. As shown in Fig. 5d, the as-fabricated MBC catalyst enables excellent catalytic performance for dissociating arbidol via activated PDS in a broad working pH range of 3–11. In MBC/PDS system, pH also holds the ability to control the catalytic reaction kinetics of arbidol degradation via tuning the adsorption capacity of catalyst interface and affecting the formation of reactive radicals (e.g., SO4 •– and •OH). As for natural pH (~5), the reaction kinetic constants (k, min−1) of catalytic arbidol decomposition is higher than that of alkaline environment (Fig. S3). The SO4 •– that is mainly from HSO5 − exists under acidic or neutral conditions. On the other side, SO5 2− with lower catalytic activity would increase at alkaline conditions and SO4 •– will consume by OH– to form •OH with relatively weak activities (Xiao et al., 2022). Howbeit the catalytic reaction dynamics of alkaline modes are slightly sluggish as compared to acidic/neutral pH, the overall arbidol decomposing efficiencies are above 82% within one hour under a universal-pH of 3 − 11 (Fig. 5d). Hence, it can be claimed that MgFe2O4-loaded biochar holds the catalytic durability in a wide range of working pH, which is superior to the traditional Fenton-like systems that are generally effective under acidic pH conditions (Peng et al., 2022). In this research, the initial pH of reaction solution is no need to adjust in the subsequent experiments with the compromise of economic benefit and pollutant removal effect. To investigate the universality of MBC-supported PDS activation, the removal efficiencies of arbidol and methyl orange are calculated to be 98.1% and 86.7% respectively (Fig. 5e). Thus, the bimetallic MgFe2O4-loaded biochar from wheat straw can be envisioned to adopt as an available catalyst in the advanced oxidation process with the objective of wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanism and theoretical calculation of arbidol degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The cross-sectional electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR), and radical quenching experiments are combined to identify the dominant active oxygen species from the arbidol degradation in the MBC/PDS system. In the meantime, the density functional theory (DFT) calculation is conducted to assist the comprehensive understanding of potential reaction mechanism. It has been widely proposed that the sulfate (SO4 •–) and (•OH) species are recognized as classical free radicals during PDS-based Fenton-like reactions (Dou et al., 2022). From the EPR results (Fig. 5 f), the apparent SO4 •– and •OH radicals are easily trapped with the existence of 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO). By adding 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidinol (TEMP) as spin-trapping agent, the weak triplet signal of TEMP-1O2 adducts is abstract to support the theory that the 1O2 species might ignite by the self-decomposition of PDS (Fig. 5 g). Since reactive species could oxidize TEMP through direct electron-transfer and then deprotons and O2 addition, the EPR result exhibits no significance differential for 1O2 (Zhao et al., 2022). Furthermore, ethanol (EtOH) is selected as the scavenger to explore the presence of SO4 •– and •OH, while tertbutyl alcohol (TBA) is adopted to quench •OH ( Fig. 6a-c). Both EtOH and TBA, even at 4000 times the molar concentrations of PDS, provides a limited inhibition on the arbidol degradation in the MBC/PDS catalytic system, implying that SO4 •– and •OH are the faint reactive species during the reaction (Zhao et al., 2022). Moreover, furfuryl alcohol (FFA) is served as the chemical probe to discern whether non-radical singlet oxygen 1O2 is formed in the MBC/PDS system. As presented in Fig. 6d, a relatively higher inhibition effect on the arbidol dissociation is observed at a FFA dosage of 20 − 30 mM, which potentially indicated that the 1O2 species is more effective than radicals (SO4 •– and •OH) for the catalytic PDS activation.

DFT calculation affords an intelligent theoretical simulation toolbox for materials screening, design, and prediction, which is carried out to distinguish the potential reaction pathways and activation mechanisms in this research. The fitting structures of pristine biochar (Fig. 6e) and MgFe2O4-loaded MBC (Fig. 6f) identify that introducing bimetallic oxides into carbon skeleton could enhance the PDS adsorption and activation via facilitating electron transfer from carbon materials to PDS. Specifically, the graphic carbon structure is inferior to sensitize PDS with a weaker charge-transfer capability and limited adsorption energy (Eads) of −1.184 eV. In contrast, the Eads of MBC combined with PDS is reduced to −2.915 eV, which indicates that their firm interaction can be readily established to endow the spontaneous generation of reactive intermediates. In this context, the optimal energy profiles of the proposed intermediates for the PDS activation generating active species are depicted in Fig. 6g. Firstly, MBC sites interacted with PDS to generate the *O3SOOSO3H complex, through an exergonic process with a calculated dissociation barrier of −0.935 eV. Subsequently, proton transfer occurred for the transition state of =MgFe−HSO5 (TS1, E 2 =−0.426 eV), leading to the formation of •OH radical and =MgFe−OSO4 (TS2) with the lower energy barriers of −1.460 eV and −1.024 eV respectively (Zhang et al., 2021b; Zhao et al., 2022). The interlinked bonds of TS2 undergo heterolytic cleavage with an acceptable barrier, which eventually produces the reactive SO4 •– and 1O2 species responsible for pollutants decomposition. According to the calculation above, the total energy barrier for reaction pathway further proves the thermodynamic feasibility of the entire reaction.

It is witnessed that the arbidol could still be degraded even if the quenching reagents are excessive during the MBC/PDS system, we audaciously hypothesize that another non-radical pathway might occur which also be accepted by the charge-transfer pathway. To further verify the contribution of direct surface electron-transfer in the arbidol decomposition with MBC/PDS system, electrochemical studies, electronic structures and transfer number are performed. As can be seen from Fig. 7, the electron-transfer from the adsorbed arbidol to PDS is verified via the measurement of PDS open-circuit potential on MBC surface. Accompanied by the addition of PDS, the circuit potential obviously increases from 0.18 V to 0.66 V (Fig. 7a), which implied that a specific intermediate is shaped between MBC and PDS to promote the electrical oxidation capacity (Peng et al., 2022). While dropping the arbidol into the above solution, the circuit potential of system is reduced suddenly (Fig. 7b), which further indicates the occurrence of charge-transfer via delivering electrons from arbidol to catalyst surface. These discoveries collectively infer that MBC functions as an “electron bridge” to beneficial the shift of electrons from arbidol to the metastable MBC/PDS* complex, rather than just a catalyst for generating radicals (Dou et al., 2022). To bolster this postulation, the number of electron transfer from MBC to PDS is theoretically calculated as 0.723 e, which is higher than that of pristine biochar (0.573 e) (Fig. 7c-d). Therefore, the as-prepared MBC could readily facilitate the direct electron migration from arbidol to PDS based on the optimal electronic structures, thereby eventually resulting in the PDS dissociation and arbidol oxidation.

On account of the aforementioned research, the synergistically radical and non-radical catalytic mechanism of arbidol decomposition in the MBC/PDS system is proposed in Fig. 8. The physical adsorption of PDS molecules are preferentially taken place on the MBC surface, thereby leading to the formation of interfacial bonding active complexes via the correlation between MBC and PDS, which captures the potential ability to mediate the electron-transfer from arbidol to PDS on the adsorbent interface. During this procedure, PDS acts as the electron acceptor while arbidol is the electron donor. Therefore, a ternary conformation of electron donor-mediator (MBC)-acceptor is steady fabricated when arbidol with plentiful electrons approached to the activated PDS molecules (Tang et al., 2018). The intense contact between MBC and PDS in the carbon framework provokes the generation of added surface-active oxygen species, such as singlet oxygen, sulfate and hydroxyl radicals. To be specific, the delocalized π electrons that generated from defective sites and lone-pair electrons in MBC functional groups (e.g., hydroxyls) can be migrated to PDS for evolving SO4 •–. The SO4 •− could react with H2O or OH− to produce •OH radicals spontaneously. On the other side, the electrophilic functional groups on the surface of carbonaceous reservoir (e.g., C−O and CO) would function as electron acceptor to seize electrons shifted from the nucleophilic PDS, enabling the formation of 1O2 species in conjunction with PDS self-decomposition (Ma et al., 2022b). These activated complexes can subsequently abstract electrons from arbidol to drive oxidation reaction, thus resulting in the efficient arbidol degradation. Taking advantages of the EPR, radical-quenching, and electrochemical experiments coupling with the DFT calculation, it can be firmly envisioned that direct electron-transfer and non-radical 1O2 are the dominated active sites responsible for arbidol decomposition.

Multi-scale biomass valorization acquires latent competence to spark the rapid growth of material science, biorefining, and environmental remediation. We ignite the revenue from dual lignocellulose valorization that allows for complicated wastewater treatment via fluorescent lignin carbon dots and bimetallic biochar catalyst in the field of selective Fe3+ sensing and arbidol pollutant elimination, respectively. Here we achieve the incorporation of DES stabilization and solvothermal functions to fractionate lignin capable of β-aryl ether and hydroxyl groups, which benefits the photoluminescence LCDs in a broad excitation span. The LCDs is facilely adopted to large-scale probe Fe3+ with a highly linear correlation of 10–50 μM. Taking advantages of the MBC-catalytic persulfate system in the wide pH, the high-performance arbidol removal efficiency with a universal concentration of 20–200 ppm is actualized via active oxygen species and surface electron transfer mechanism. Relying on this cascade technique with scalable acceptance to various lignocellulose species, it can be extended to generate multifunctional materials for striding the threshold of complete biomass conversion, thereby broadening the environmental frontiers and proceeds of biorefining towards the “waste-treats-pollutant” concept.

Guo Jun: Formal analysis, Data curation, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Xu Jikun: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Liu Xinyan: Writing – review & editing. Dai Lin: Writing – review & editing. Zhang Chuntao: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Xiao Xiao: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Resources. Huo Kaifu: Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express their gratitude for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32171728 and 31700511), and the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018T110770).

ce:section.ce:para:
Biomass-related technologies hold great promise to tackle the double crises of environmental pollution and energy scarcity. The environmental implication of this research is summarized as the following standpoint:
1)
Towards a novel “waste-treats-pollutant” concept, we successfully propose dual lignocellulose valorization that guides biomass industries to serve environmental engineering (e.g., heavy metal ions and organic contaminants) via a green DES-driven cascade scheme;
2)
DES deconstruction of wheat straw potentially facilitates LCDs preparation and MBC-catalytic persulfate activation to treat complicated wastewater (i.e., Fe3+ sensing and arbidol elimination), which maximizes the functional versatility and environmental benefits of available biorefinery.
